steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage sets up a direct contrast: in Massey's work, abstraction makes Indigenous identity invisible (viewers cannot detect it). Then "In contrast" introduces Earles, who builds abstraction from traditional tribal motifs. The blank must complete the logical opposite — what effect does Earles's tribal-grounded abstraction produce, in contrast to Massey's invisibility?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'rendering Indigenous identity more legible than it is in Untitled.': If Earles's abstraction is built from tribal motifs, the cultural source is embedded in the work and thus recognizable — "more legible" is the direct opposite of Massey's invisibility and completes the "In contrast" structure correctly. ✓
  • Choice B — 'asserting the Indigenous origins of motifs associated with Abstract Expressionism.': The passage says Abstract Expressionism was dominated by European American artists, not that its motifs were Indigenous in origin. Earles works from Caddo tribal ceramics, not from Abstract Expressionism's repertoire — so this misidentifies the source of his motifs. ✗
  • Choice C — 'challenging the dominance of European American artists within Abstract Expressionism.': The passage notes that dominance as historical context, but the discussion of Earles is about the effect of abstraction on legibility, not about a power challenge within an art movement. This changes the topic. ✗
  • Choice D — 'reconciling Indigenous and European American influences that coexist uneasily in Untitled.': The passage does not describe Untitled as a site of uneasily coexisting influences — it says viewers simply cannot detect Indigenous identity in it at all. This fabricates a tension not in the passage. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
